bottlenecks.location: GEMM-heavy transformer compute: aten::mm, aten::addmm, and Ampere SGEMM kernels dominate the step
bottlenecks.notes: This is the largest single category by CUDA time. The estimated headroom is bounded because matmul is already using vendor GEMM kernels; the remaining gap is from kernel efficiency and overall compute intensity, not from eliminating the work.

bottlenecks.location: Elementwise tensor ops and fused-unfused pointwise work
bottlenecks.notes: Pointwise work is a meaningful secondary cost, but it is smaller than matmul and typically less dominant in end-to-end step time.
Optimizer overhead is non-trivial relative to the short 22.8 ms median step time, but still clearly smaller than the forward/backward GEMM workload.

bottlenecks.location: Manual causal attention path, including aten::bmm
bottlenecks.notes: Attention is present but not the dominant cost in this profile; most time is elsewhere in matmul and pointwise/optimizer work.
The input pipeline is visible but very small relative to compute; it is not a primary limiter in this run.

bottleneck_report.summary: This run is overwhelmingly compute-bound on a single A100: matmul alone accounts for 46.2% of step time (11 ms of a 22.8 ms median step), with additional time in elementwise ops (15.6%), AdamW (11.1%), and attention (6.5%). Memory pressure is negligible at 1.43 GB peak on an 80 GB GPU, and dataloader stalls are under 1% of time, so the step is dominated by GPU kernel execution rather than input, communication, or capacity limits.
bottleneck_report.time_breakdown_narrative: The median step time is 22.8 ms, and the kernel breakdown shows that most of that time is spent in GPU compute. Matmul is the largest bucket at 46.2% of step time, or about 11 ms/step, with the top kernels being aten::mm (12.8% of total CUDA time), aten::addmm (7.1%), and several Ampere SGEMM kernels. Elementwise work is the next largest category at 15.6% (4 ms/step), which is consistent with the presence of aten::mul and vectorized elementwise kernels in the top-kernel list. Optimizer.step#AdamW.step contributes 11.1% (3 ms/step), while attention is only 6.5% (1 ms/step) and normalization is 4.2% (1 ms/step). The dataloader stall rate is only 0.916%, so input waiting is minor. Memory peak is 1.43 GB on an 80 GB A100, implying 98.2% headroom and ruling out memory capacity pressure as a bottleneck.
unstructured_notes: The profile is from a GPT-style character-level language model with manual causal attention and fp32 precision.
No distributed communication is present (world size 1), so there is no all-reduce or synchronization bottleneck in this run.

The reported headroom is limited by the fact that the dominant costs are already core GEMM kernels; the largest estimated gains come from reducing the matmul share, but not all of that time is removable.
Because several model hyperparameters are unknown from the script alone, the exact split between forward, backward, and optimizer work is partially inferred from kernel names and category totals.
